title: Three ways to write a function
instruction:
JavaScript has three function syntaxes. They are mostly interchangeable — but they differ in hoisting and `this` binding.

Run this to see all three do the same thing.
successMessage: Three syntaxes, same result. Use arrow functions by default (modern, concise). Use function declarations when you need hoisting or a named function in stack traces.

title: Arrow functions — compact syntax

codeChecks.message: Rewrite double as arrow: n => n * 2
Rewrite add as arrow: (a, b) => a + b

successMessage: Compact arrows everywhere. user => user.name is cleaner than function(user) { return user.name; } — and you'll see this in every map/filter/sort call.

title: Parameters and return
instruction:
Functions can take multiple parameters and return any value. A function with no `return` statement returns `undefined`.

Write `bmi(weight, height)` that returns the Body Mass Index: `weight / (height * height)`.

codeChecks.message: Use return to send back the result.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: 22.9 — correct BMI. return sends the value back to the caller. Without return, the function would give undefined and toFixed would crash.

title: Default parameters

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Defaults fill in when arguments are omitted. Notice { name, price, inStock, category } in the return — that's shorthand property syntax. When key name = variable name, you don't need the colon.

title: Rest parameters — any number of args
hint: function max(...nums) { return nums.reduce((a, b) => a > b ? a : b); }

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Rest params make functions flexible — accept any number of arguments. The rest array is a regular array so all array methods work on it.

title: Callbacks — functions as arguments
instruction:
Functions are values in JavaScript — you can pass them as arguments to other functions.

A function that takes another function as an argument is called a **higher-order function**. The passed function is a **callback**.

You've already used this: `array.map(fn)`, `array.filter(fn)` — both take callbacks.

Write a `processData` function that accepts data and a transformer callback.
successMessage: Same processData, three different callbacks, three different results. Higher-order functions + callbacks = functional programming. map, filter, reduce are all higher-order functions.

title: Pure functions — same input, same output
instruction:
A **pure function** always returns the same result for the same inputs and has no side effects (doesn't modify anything outside itself).

Pure functions are predictable, testable, and safe to use anywhere.

Fix `addToCart` — it mutates the original cart (impure). Make it pure by returning a new cart.

codeChecks.message: Return a new array: return [...cart, item]
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: The original cart is untouched — a pure function. React state management requires pure functions. Redux, Zustand, all state libraries depend on immutability.

title: IIFE — run once immediately
expectedOutput: secret is not accessible outside IIFE
successMessage: secret is completely hidden. IIFE was the pre-ES6 module pattern — bundle everything in an IIFE and expose only what you choose. Now we use proper ES modules (Chapter 16).

title: Build a validation library
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build a small validation library:
- `isEmail(str)` — returns true if string contains '@' and a '.' after '@'
- `isLength(str, min, max)` — returns true if length is within range
- `isInRange(num, min, max)` — returns true if number is within range
- `validate(value, ...rules)` — takes a value and any number of rule functions, returns true only if ALL pass

Test validate with multiple rules at once.

codeChecks.message: Use .every() so all rules must pass.

successMessage: A composable validation library! validate(email, isEmail, v => isLength(v, 5, 100)) — chain any rules. This pattern is exactly how libraries like Zod and Joi work. Chapter 9 done!
